does: This paper evaluates expert perceptions of mitigation strategies for systemic risks posed by general-purpose AI models. The researchers conducted a survey of 76 experts across diverse domains including AI safety, critical infrastructure, democratic processes, CBRN risks, and bias and discrimination. They identified 27 potential mitigation measures through literature review and assessed expert judgments on their effectiveness and technical feasibility. The study finds that a range of mitigations are perceived as both effective and feasible, with three measures emerging as particularly promising: safety incident reporting and security information sharing, third-party pre-deployment model audits, and pre-deployment risk assessments.
connects: This work builds on previous research proposing theoretical frameworks for AI risk mitigation while addressing acknowledged gaps in understanding which measures experts actually consider effective and implementable. It extends risk assessment literature from traditional domains like nuclear and biosecurity into the general-purpose AI context, adapting expert elicitation methodologies from critical infrastructure and safety studies. The paper responds to growing concern about systemic AI risks by combining perspectives from multiple established fields—AI safety, information security, policy, and domain-specific risk management—to move beyond speculation toward empirically grounded expert consensus on practical mitigation strategies.
enables: This work establishes a foundation for evidence-based AI governance by identifying mitigation strategies with cross-domain expert support. The identification of high-consensus measures like pre-deployment audits and incident reporting enables policymakers and organizations to prioritize implementation efforts and resource allocation. The framework can guide the design of specific regulatory and corporate policies for AI systems. Future work can build on these findings to develop detailed implementation protocols, establish technical standards for third-party audits, create incident reporting infrastructure, and assess real-world effectiveness of deployed mitigations. The expert consensus may also enable comparative analysis of different jurisdictional approaches to AI governance.
